title: 怪钟疑案
summary:
During a visit to Whitehall House, Hercule Poirot explains to Colin Lamb that he has been seeking intellectual stimulation through research and problem-solving. Poirot recounts a minor mystery he solved involving three dry orange peels found on his umbrella stand, which led to the discovery that a new cleaning lady had violated rules by bringing her child to work. He also mentions applying his methods to real-life unsolved mysteries, such as the Charles Bravo case and the case of Constance Kent, claiming he has identified the motives and culprits in those instances.

The conversation shifts to a critique of crime literature. Poirot praises "The Case of the Living Dummy" for its atmosphere and "The Adventures of Arsenè Lupin" for its humor, while describing "The Secret of the Yellow Room" as a masterpiece of logical reasoning. Regarding contemporary authors, he criticizes Ariadne Oliver’s early works for being unrealistic and her portrayal of Finnish characters as inaccurate, though he notes her later improvement in describing police procedures. He discusses Cyril Quin, noting that while the plots are repetitive and rely on intricate alibis involving train and bus schedules, he enjoys the process of unraveling them. Conversely, he dismisses the horror novelist Gary Gregson for creating chaotic and unbelievable plots.

Discussing American writers, Poirot praises Florence Eckle for her methodical pacing but criticizes the frequent, unnecessary inclusion of alcohol consumption in American crime fiction. While he dismisses the hardboiled genre's focus on violence, he concludes that American crime novels are often more cunning and imaginative than British ones, citing Louisa O'Malley as an example.
